figures.caption: Figure 2: Dense→pruned feature survival (MNN at τ = 0.7). Wanda consistently preserves more features than magnitude pruning across all models and sparsity levels.
Table A.1: Summary of experimental runs by tier. Seeds column shows number of SAE random seeds per run.

Figure 4: Feature survival rate by firing rate quintile (τ = 0.7). Across all models and pruning methods, rare features (Q1) survive substantially better than frequent ones (Q5). Lines represent different pruning method–sparsity combinations.

Figure 1: Seed stability (MNN rate at τ = 0.7) as a function of sparsity. Error bars show standard deviation across seed pairs. While absolute MNN rates are low (2–4%), the pattern of degradation with sparsity is consistent.

Figure 3: Transferability of pre-trained (official) SAEs to pruned model activations. FVU is plotted against sparsity for Gemma 3 1B and Gemma 2 2B, separated by pruning method. Wanda-pruned activations remain well within the reconstructable range.

Figure 6: Mean KL divergence upon single-feature ablation for robust versus fragile features. Across all models and conditions, robust and fragile features show nearly identical causal effects, indicating a dissociation between geometric survival and causal importance.

Table 2: Transferability: FVU of official (dense-trained) SAEs applied to pruned model activations. Lower FVU indicates better reconstruction. Wanda-pruned activations remain well-reconstructable up to 50% sparsity.
Table 4: Mean KL divergence upon single-feature ablation. Robust and fragile features show nearly identical causal effects across all models and conditions. ∆ is the absolute percent difference.

Figure 5: Feature fragility by firing rate, separated by pruning method. Blue: magnitude pruning; orange: Wanda. The monotonic decline from Q1 (rare) to Q5 (frequent) is robust across all models, methods, and sparsity levels.
Table B.1: SAE reconstruction quality (FVU) averaged over seeds. The large majority of SAEs achieved near-complete feature utilization with our training procedure.

Table 1: WikiText-2 perplexity across models, pruning methods, and sparsity levels. Lower is better. Gemma 2 dense perplexity is anomalously high (likely due to context length mismatch); relative differences remain informative. Catastrophic values (> 105) are abbreviated.

Table 3: Feature survival rate by firing rate quintile (τ = 0.7). In all conditions, rare features (Q1) survive at substantially higher rates than frequent features (Q5). The ratio column shows the Q1/Q5 survival advantage.
